nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-flowing or still swamp habitats with murky, darkly-stained (tannin-rich) water; dense submerged and riparian vegetation. Type locality is a sago swamp in the Mimika District near Timika, Papua Province, at 45–60 m elevation. Closely associated with the Aikwa and Iweka River systems in south-central New Guinea.

decorPreferences: dense fine-leaved planting (Java moss, hornwort, water sprite) for spawning sites
floating plants (frogbit, salvinia) to diffuse surface light and reduce skittishness
driftwood and dried leaves for tannin release and natural feel
low or gentle flow — avoid strong currents

diet.notes: In the wild, the Red Neon Rainbowfish feeds primarily on zooplankton, phytoplankton, small insects, and tiny invertebrates — making it essentially a microinvertivore. In captivity, live and frozen micro-foods are strongly preferred: baby brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops, moina, and microworms all work well. Crushed high-quality flake and micro pellets can serve as a staple but the species benefits greatly from regular live or frozen supplementation. The mouth is small, so food particles must be appropriately sized. Fry require infusoria or paramecium for the first five days before they are large enough to accept baby brine shrimp.

goodWith: other Pseudomugil species (similar size and parameters)
small peaceful tetras (Ember tetra, Green neon tetra)
dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina — adults safe; fry may be eaten)
avoidWith: faster or larger fish that outcompete for food
boisterous community fish (tiger barbs, larger danios)

imageCandidates.notes: Male specimen; FishBase species image for Pseudomugil luminatus. Verify availability and license before use.

summary: The Red Neon Rainbowfish (Pseudomugil luminatus) is a small, vividly coloured blue-eye from the blackwater sago swamps of West Papua and southern New Guinea. Males display a rich red-violet body, a neon-blue dorsal stripe, and elongated filamentous fin rays. Adults reach around 4 cm and do best in groups of eight or more in a soft-water, planted nano aquarium.
careNotes: The Red Neon Rainbowfish was formally described in 2016 from sago swamp habitat near Timika, in the Mimika District of Papua Province, Indonesia. Museum specimens had been collected since 1997 but were misidentified as P. paskai; the species circulated in the trade from around 2011 under the informal names 'Pseudomugil sp. Red Neon' and 'Pseudomugil sp. paskai Red Form' before it received its formal description. It is assessed as IUCN Endangered, with a very restricted wild range centred on the Aikwa and Iweka River systems of south-central New Guinea. A biologically mature aquarium is essential before introducing this species — it tends to be sensitive to poor water quality and sudden parameter swings. Soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 1–10) with low flow best replicates its blackwater swamp origin. Adding dried leaves (such as Indian almond or catappa) or driftwood to release tannins is generally beneficial. Groups of at least 8–10 individuals are recommended to distribute male display aggression and reduce individual stress; a species tank or nano biotope with similarly-sized soft-water fish tends to suit it best. Males are conspicuously coloured — intense red-violet body, neon-blue stripe along the dorsal midline, and filamentous extensions on the second dorsal fin spine and first pelvic fin ray — and typically engage in near-constant fin-flickering display toward one another. Females are smaller, paler, and shorter-finned. The Red Neon Rainbowfish is sometimes confused with the Forktail Blue-Eye (P. furcatus) and Gertrude's Blue-Eye (P. gertrudae); P. luminatus is distinguished by its vivid red coloration, shorter body of around 4 cm or less, and its West Papuan lowland swamp origin. Feed primarily with small live and frozen foods such as baby brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops, and microworms; crushed micro-pellets and flake are accepted but live foods tend to improve conditioning and coloration. The species has a short natural lifespan of around one to two years in the wild; captive specimens kept in good conditions may reach three years.
breedingNotes: The Red Neon Rainbowfish is a continuous egg-scatterer with no parental care. Well-conditioned males display intensely to females, and spawning can occur on a near-daily basis when the group is in good condition. Eggs are deposited individually on fine-leaved plants, java moss, or spawning mops; each egg measures about 1 mm and has adhesive threads that anchor it to plant material. Incubation takes approximately 10–14 days at 24–26°C. Fry hatch very small and require microscopic live foods such as paramecium or infusoria for the first five days before they can accept baby brine shrimp nauplii and microworms. Fry tend to grow relatively slowly. Adults should be removed after spawning, or very dense plant cover provided, as eggs and fry will otherwise be eaten. Spawning frequency and egg viability generally improve at temperatures in the upper part of the recommended range (24–26°C). Breeding is considered moderately difficult due to the need for live foods, soft water conditioning, and careful fry nutrition.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Pseudomugil sp. 'Red Neon'
primary.notes: Primary reference. Provided adult size (30–35 mm SL), temperature (18–26°C), pH (6.5–7.5), hardness (36–215 ppm ≈ 2–12 dGH), minimum tank footprint (45×30 cm, ~41 L), diet, breeding method, lifespan, native range (Timika, Mimika Regency, Papua), habitat (murky riparian swamp), and compatibility. Note: page covers the pre-description 'sp. Red Neon' trade form, which corresponds to P. luminatus following Allen et al. 2016. A dedicated P. luminatus page was not live as of access date.

additional.name: FishBase — Pseudomugil luminatus
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Family Pseudomugilidae, Order Atheriniformes), max size (1.9 cm SL per FishBase — likely underrepresented; hobbyist sources consistently report up to 4 cm TL), native range (Indonesia, Papua Province), IUCN Endangered status (EN, assessed December 2019), and description year (2016, Allen, Unmack & Hadiaty). Trophic level 3.1 consistent with microinvertivore diet.

additional.name: ANGFA — Pseudomugil luminatus (rainbowfish.angfa.org.au)
additional.notes: Provided type locality detail (sago swamp, Mimika District, near Timika; 4°40.669'S, 136°57.046'E), habitat description (clear, darkly stained slow-flowing swamps at 45–60 m elevation), wild water parameters (temp 26–28°C, pH 4.8–7.2, conductivity 7–126 µS/cm confirming very soft water), Aikwa/Iweka River system location, and species entry history in the hobby (circulated from ~2011 as 'Red Neon' before 2016 formal description).

additional.name: Aquatic Arts — Red Neon Blue Eye Rainbowfish
additional.notes: Trade care summary. Confirmed adult size 3–3.8 cm TL, temperature 18–26°C, pH 6.5–7.5, hardness 12–15 dKH (used as dGH approximation cross-check), minimum 10-gallon tank, omnivorous diet accepting dry foods with live/frozen supplementation. Noted species timidity and need for groups; compatibility with adult dwarf shrimp.

additional.name: Aquadiction — Red Neon Blue Eye Rainbowfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Independent care guide. Confirmed max size ~4 cm, temperature 17–26°C, pH 6.5–7.5, gH 12–15, soft water preference, lifespan up to 2 years, omnivore feeding on zooplankton/invertebrates supplemented with live foods, egg-deposition breeding with ~10-day incubation, fry requiring paramecium initially. Family listed as Melanotaeniidae (likely an error; Pseudomugilidae is correct per FishBase and original description).

fish.breedingNotes: A continuous egg-scatterer that deposits adhesive eggs daily among fine plants or spawning mops. Incubation takes approximately 10–14 days. Fry require infusoria or paramecium for the first five days before graduating to baby brine shrimp. Soft, warm water and live-food conditioning are needed for consistent spawning.
